title: Creating Alluvial Diagrams
text: Alluvial diagram variant Parallel Coordinates Plot (PCP) categorical variables. Variables assigned vertical axes parallel. Values represented blocks axis. Observations represented alluvia (sing. “alluvium”) spanning across axes. create alluvial diagrams function alluvial(). Let us use Titanic dataset example. table, need convert data frame create alluvial diagram:  four variables: Class ship passanger occupied Sex passenger Age passenger Whether passenger Survived. Vertical sizes blocks proportional frequency, widths alluvia. Alluvia represent combinations values variables dataset. default vertical order alluvia determined alphabetical ordering values variable lexicographically (last variable changes first) drawn bottom top. example, color determined passengers’ survival status, .e. passenger survived represented orange alluvia. Alluvial diagrams useful reading various conditional uncoditional distributions multivariate dataset. example, can see : Crew survived – majority height Crew category covered grey alluvia. Majortity Crew adult men. Almost women 1st Class survive. women survive come mostly 3rd class.
code: tit <- as.data.frame(Titanic, stringsAsFactors = FALSE) head(tit) ##   Class    Sex   Age Survived Freq ## 1   1st   Male Child       No    0 ## 2   2nd   Male Child       No    0 ## 3   3rd   Male Child       No   35 ## 4  Crew   Male Child       No    0 ## 5   1st Female Child       No    0 ## 6   2nd Female Child       No    0 alluvial(   tit[,1:4],    freq=tit$Freq,   col = ifelse(tit$Survived == "Yes", "orange", "grey"),   border = ifelse(tit$Survived == "Yes", "orange", "grey"),   hide = tit$Freq == 0,   cex = 0.7 )

title: Creating Alluvial Diagrams
text: Minimal use requires supplying data frame(s) first argument, vector frequencies freq argument. default alluvia drawn using mildly transparent gray. Two variables Class Survived:  Three variables Sex, Class, Survived:
code: # Survival status and Class tit %>% group_by(Class, Survived) %>%   summarise(n = sum(Freq)) -> tit2d ## `summarise()` has regrouped the output. ## ℹ Summaries were computed grouped by Class and Survived. ## ℹ Output is grouped by Class. ## ℹ Use `summarise(.groups = "drop_last")` to silence this message. ## ℹ Use `summarise(.by = c(Class, Survived))` for per-operation grouping ##   (`?dplyr::dplyr_by`) instead. alluvial(tit2d[,1:2], freq=tit2d$n) # Survival status, Sex, and Class tit %>% group_by(Sex, Class, Survived) %>%   summarise(n = sum(Freq)) -> tit3d ## `summarise()` has regrouped the output. ## ℹ Summaries were computed grouped by Sex, Class, and Survived. ## ℹ Output is grouped by Sex and Class. ## ℹ Use `summarise(.groups = "drop_last")` to silence this message. ## ℹ Use `summarise(.by = c(Sex, Class, Survived))` for per-operation grouping ##   (`?dplyr::dplyr_by`) instead. alluvial(tit3d[,1:3], freq=tit3d$n)

title: Creating Alluvial Diagrams
text: several ways customize alluvial diagrams alluvial() following sections illustrate probably common usecases.

title: Creating Alluvial Diagrams
text: Colors alluvia can customized col, border alpha arguments. example:
code: alluvial(   tit3d[,1:3],    freq=tit3d$n,   col = ifelse( tit3d$Sex == "Female", "pink", "lightskyblue"),   border = "grey",   alpha = 0.7,   blocks=FALSE )

title: Creating Alluvial Diagrams
text: alluvial sometimes desirable omit plotting alluvia. frequently case larger datasets lot combinations values variables associated small frequencies, even 0s. Alluvia can hidden argument hide expecting logical vector length equal number rows data. Alluvia hide FALSE plotted. example, hide alluvia frequency less 150:  skips drawing alluvia corresponding following rows tit data frame: can see gaps e.g. “Yes” “” category blocks Survived axis. rather omit rows plot alltogether (.e. gaps), need filter data used alluvial().
code: alluvial(tit2d[,1:2], freq=tit2d$n, hide=tit2d$n < 150) tit2d %>% select(Class, Survived, n) %>%   filter(n < 150) ## # A tibble: 2 × 3 ## # Groups:   Class [2] ##   Class Survived     n ##   <chr> <chr>    <dbl> ## 1 1st   No         122 ## 2 2nd   Yes        118

title: Creating Alluvial Diagrams
text: default alluvia plotted sequence determined row order dataset. determines alluvia plotted “top” others. Consider simple data: three rows, three alluvia:  Note keep colors matched way alluvia reverse col argument . Instead reordering data keeping track arguments plotting order can adjusted layer argument:  value layer passed order possible use logical vectors e.g. want put flows top. example, Titanic data put alluvia survivors top can:  First layer one top, second layer first . Consequently, example , Survived == "" ordered Survived == "Yes" former latter.
code: d <- data.frame(   x = c(1, 2, 3),   y = c(3 ,2, 1),   freq=c(1,1,1) ) d ##   x y freq ## 1 1 3    1 ## 2 2 2    1 ## 3 3 1    1 alluvial(d[,1:2], freq=d$freq, col=1:3, alpha=1) # Reversing the order alluvial(d[ 3:1, 1:2 ], freq=d$freq, col=3:1, alpha=1) alluvial(d[,1:2], freq=d$freq, col=1:3, alpha=1,           layer=3:1) alluvial(tit3d[,1:3], freq=tit3d$n,           col = ifelse( tit3d$Survived == "Yes", "orange", "grey" ),          alpha = 0.8,          layer = tit3d$Survived == "No" )

title: Creating Alluvial Diagrams
text: default alluvial() orders values axis alphabetic order. happens irrespectively ordering observations plotted dataset. possible override default ordering transforming variables interest factors custom ordering levels. Consider following example data: Plotting alluvial() default settings give:  Let’s change order categories : Color axis (bottom): green, blue, orange, red. Luminance axis reversed. variable create factor vector unique values variable sorted way want passed levels argument factor(): … plot  Another version recognizing data Color nested Temperature. Different axis order gives clearer picture data structure.
code: d <- data.frame(   col = c("#A6CEE3", "#1F78B4", "#B2DF8A", "#33A02C", "#FB9A99",            "#E31A1C", "#FDBF6F", "#FF7F00"), # from RColorBrewer Paired palette   Temperature = rep(c("cool", "hot"), each=4),   Luminance = rep(c("bright", "dark"), 4),   Color = rep(c("blue", "green", "red", "orange"), each=2) ) %>%   mutate(     n = 1   ) d ##       col Temperature Luminance  Color n ## 1 #A6CEE3        cool    bright   blue 1 ## 2 #1F78B4        cool      dark   blue 1 ## 3 #B2DF8A        cool    bright  green 1 ## 4 #33A02C        cool      dark  green 1 ## 5 #FB9A99         hot    bright    red 1 ## 6 #E31A1C         hot      dark    red 1 ## 7 #FDBF6F         hot    bright orange 1 ## 8 #FF7F00         hot      dark orange 1 alluvial(   select(d, Temperature, Luminance, Color),   freq=d$n,   col = d$col,   alpha=0.9 ) d <- d %>%   mutate(     Color_f = factor(Color, levels=c("green", "blue", "red", "orange")),     Luminance_f = factor(Luminance, levels=c("dark", "bright"))   ) alluvial(   select(d, Temperature, Luminance_f, Color_f),   freq=d$n,   col = d$col,   alpha=0.9 ) alluvial(   select(d, Temperature, Color_f, Luminance_f),   freq=d$n,   col = d$col,   alpha=0.9 )

title: Creating Alluvial Diagrams
text: feature experimental! Usually order variables (axes) rather unimportant. However, particular two variables next facilitates analyzing dependency two variables. alluvial diagrams ordering variables determines vertical plotting order alluvia. vertical order, together setting blocks FALSE, can used turn category blocks stacked barcharts. Consider two versions subsets Titanic data differ order variables. x Sex-Age-Survived-n y Survived-Age-Sex-n. color alluvia according first axis, category blocks Age Survived become barcharts showing relative frequencies Men Women within categories Age Survived.  Now can see example little bit girls boys (category Age == "Child") Among surviors roughly number Men Women. Argument ordering can used fully customize ordering alluvium axis without need reorder axes . feature experimental can easily break things. expects list numeric vectors NULLs one variable data: Value NULL change default order corresponding axis. numeric vector length equal number rows data determines vertical order alluvia corresponding axis. example:  list passed ordering three elements corresponding Survived, Age, Sex respectively (’s order variables y). elements list Call order sorting alluvia Survived axis. alluvia need sorted according Survived first (otherwise categories “Yes” “” destroyed) according Sex second. Call order sorting alluvia Age axis. alluvia need sorted according Age first Sex second. NULL leaves default ordering Sex axis. example alluvia colored sex (red=Female, blue=Male) survival status (bright=survived, dark=survive). category block stacked barchart showing relative freuquencies man/women /survive. alluvia reordered last axis (Age) Sex categories next (red together blue together):
code: tit %>% group_by(Sex, Age, Survived) %>%   summarise( n= sum(Freq)) -> x ## `summarise()` has regrouped the output. ## ℹ Summaries were computed grouped by Sex, Age, and Survived. ## ℹ Output is grouped by Sex and Age. ## ℹ Use `summarise(.groups = "drop_last")` to silence this message. ## ℹ Use `summarise(.by = c(Sex, Age, Survived))` for per-operation grouping ##   (`?dplyr::dplyr_by`) instead. tit %>% group_by(Survived, Age, Sex) %>%   summarise( n= sum(Freq)) -> y ## `summarise()` has regrouped the output. ## ℹ Summaries were computed grouped by Survived, Age, and Sex. ## ℹ Output is grouped by Survived and Age. ## ℹ Use `summarise(.groups = "drop_last")` to silence this message. ## ℹ Use `summarise(.by = c(Survived, Age, Sex))` for per-operation grouping ##   (`?dplyr::dplyr_by`) instead. alluvial(x[,1:3], freq=x$n,           col = ifelse(x$Sex == "Male", "orange", "grey"),          alpha = 0.8,          blocks=FALSE ) alluvial(y[,1:3], freq=y$n,           # col = RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(8, "Set1"),          col = ifelse(y$Sex == "Male", "orange", "grey"),          alpha = 0.8,          blocks = FALSE,          ordering = list(            order(y$Survived, y$Sex == "Male"),            order(y$Age, y$Sex == "Male"),            NULL          ) ) pal <- c("red4", "lightskyblue4", "red", "lightskyblue")  tit %>%   mutate(     ss = paste(Survived, Sex),     k = pal[ match(ss, sort(unique(ss))) ]   ) -> tit   alluvial(tit[,c(4,2,3)], freq=tit$Freq,          hide = tit$Freq < 10,          col = tit$k,          border = tit$k,          blocks=FALSE,          ordering = list(            NULL,            NULL,            order(tit$Age, tit$Sex )           ) )

title: Creating Alluvial Diagrams

code: @Manual{,   title = {{alluvial}: R Package for Creating Alluvial Diagrams},   author = {Michal Bojanowski and Robin Edwards},   year = {2017},   note = {R package version: 0.2-0},   url = {https://github.com/mbojan/alluvial}, }

title: Alluvial Diagrams
text: alluvial diagrams? See example: Wikipedia blog post showing-package discussion CrossValidated package use base R graphics (R Core Team 2021) package. Grammar Graphics implementation see ggalluvial (Brunson Read 2020; Brunson 2020).

title: Alluvial Diagrams
text: Alluvial diagram datasets::Titanic data made alluvial(). Notice category block becomes stacked barchart showing relative frequency survivors.  Alluvial diagram multiple time series / cross-sectional data based alluvial::Refugees data made alluvial_ts().
code: tit <- tibble::as_tibble(Titanic)  tit %>% head() %>% knitr::kable() alluvial(     select(tit, Survived, Sex, Age, Class),     freq=tit$n,     col = ifelse(tit$Survived == "Yes", "orange", "grey"),     border = ifelse(tit$Survived == "Yes", "orange", "grey"),     layer = tit$Survived != "Yes",     alpha = 0.8,     blocks=FALSE   ) Refugees %>% head() %>% knitr::kable() set.seed(39) # for nice colours cols <- hsv(h = sample(1:10/10), s = sample(3:12)/15, v = sample(3:12)/15)  alluvial_ts(Refugees, wave = .3, ygap = 5, col = cols, plotdir = 'centred', alpha=.9,             grid = TRUE, grid.lwd = 5, xmargin = 0.2, lab.cex = .7, xlab = '',             ylab = '', border = NA, axis.cex = .8, leg.cex = .7,             leg.col='white',              title = "UNHCR-recognised refugees\nTop 10 countries (2003-13)\n")

title: Alluvial Diagrams
text: Install stable version CRAN using development version GitHub using remotes::install_github():

title: Refugees data — Refugees
text: Top 10 countries/territories origin (excluding "Various") period 2003-13 UNHCR statistics "Persons recognized refugees 1951 UN Convention/1967 Protocol, 1969 OAU Convention, accordance UNHCR Statute, persons granted complementary form protection granted temporary protection."

title: Refugees data — Refugees
text: Data frame following columns: country Country territory origin year Year (2003-13) refugees Persons recognized refugees 1951 UN Convention, etc..

title: Refugees data — Refugees
text: http://data.un.org/Data.aspx?d=UNHCR&f=indID%3aType-Ref

title: Alluvial diagram — alluvial
text: Drawing alluvial diagrams, also known parallel set plots.

title: Alluvial diagram — alluvial
code: alluvial(   ...,   freq,   col = "gray",   border = 0,   layer,   hide = FALSE,   alpha = 0.5,   gap.width = 0.05,   xw = 0.1,   cw = 0.1,   blocks = TRUE,   ordering = NULL,   axis_labels = NULL,   mar = c(2, 1, 1, 1),   cex = par("cex"),   xlim_offset = c(0, 0),   ylim_offset = c(0, 0),   cex.axis = par("cex.axis"),   axes = TRUE,   ann = TRUE,   title = NULL )

title: Alluvial diagram — alluvial
text: ... vectors data frames, number observations freq numeric, vector frequencies length number observations col vector colors stripes border vector border colors stripes layer numeric, order drawing stripes hide logical, particular stripe plotted alpha numeric, vector transparency stripes gap.width numeric, relative width inter-category gaps xw numeric, distance set axis control points xspline cw numeric, width category axis blocks logical, whether use blocks tie flows together category, versus contiguous ribbons (also admits character value "bookends") ordering list numeric vectors allowing reorder alluvia axis separately, see Examples axis_labels character, labels axes, defaults variable names data mar numeric, plot margins par cex, cex.axis numeric, scaling fonts category labels axis labels respectively. See par. xlim_offset, ylim_offset numeric vectors length 2, passed xlim ylim plot, allow adjusting limits plotting region axes logical, whether draw axes, defaults TRUE ann logical, whether draw annotations: category labels. Defaults TRUE title character, plot title

title: Alluvial diagram — alluvial
text: Invisibly list elements: endpoints data frame data locations stripes columns: ... Vectors/data frames supplied alluvial ... define axes .bottom,.top Y locations bottom top coordinates respectively stripes originate axis .axis .axis Axis number counting left  category_midpoints List vectors Y locations category block midpoints. alluvium_midpoints data frame location midpoints alluvium segement columns: ... Vectors/data frames supplied alluvial ... .axis_from, .axis_to IDs axes segment originates goes .x, .y X Y locations alluvium midpoints .slope (approximate) slope alluvium midpoint

title: Alluvial diagram — alluvial
text: Please mind API planned change compatible   dplyr verbs.

title: Alluvial diagram — alluvial
code: # Titanic data tit <- as.data.frame(Titanic)  # 2d tit2d <- aggregate( Freq ~ Class + Survived, data=tit, sum) alluvial( tit2d[,1:2], freq=tit2d$Freq, xw=0.0, alpha=0.8,          gap.width=0.1, col= "steelblue", border="white",          layer = tit2d$Survived != "Yes" )   alluvial( tit2d[,1:2], freq=tit2d$Freq,           hide=tit2d$Freq < 150,          xw=0.0, alpha=0.8,          gap.width=0.1, col= "steelblue", border="white",          layer = tit2d$Survived != "Yes" )   # 3d tit3d <- aggregate( Freq ~ Class + Sex + Survived, data=tit, sum)  alluvial(tit3d[,1:3], freq=tit3d$Freq, alpha=1, xw=0.2,          col=ifelse( tit3d$Survived == "No", "red", "gray"),          layer = tit3d$Sex != "Female",          border="white")    # 4d alluvial( tit[,1:4], freq=tit$Freq, border=NA,          hide = tit$Freq < quantile(tit$Freq, .50),          col=ifelse( tit$Class == "3rd" & tit$Sex == "Male", "red", "gray") )   # 3d example with custom ordering # Reorder "Sex" axis according to survival status ord <- list(NULL, with(tit3d, order(Sex, Survived)), NULL) alluvial(tit3d[,1:3], freq=tit3d$Freq, alpha=1, xw=0.2,          col=ifelse( tit3d$Survived == "No", "red", "gray"),          layer = tit3d$Sex != "Female",          border="white", ordering=ord)   # Possible blocks options for (blocks in c(TRUE, FALSE, "bookends")) {          # Elaborate alluvial diagram from main examples file     alluvial( tit[, 1:4], freq = tit$Freq, border = NA,               hide = tit$Freq < quantile(tit$Freq, .50),               col = ifelse( tit$Class == "3rd" & tit$Sex == "Male",                             "red", "gray" ),               blocks = blocks ) }      # Data returned x <- alluvial( tit2d[,1:2], freq=tit2d$Freq, xw=0.0, alpha=0.8,           gap.width=0.1, col= "steelblue", border="white",           layer = tit2d$Survived != "Yes" ) points( rep(1, 16), x$endpoints[[1]], col="green") points( rep(2, 16), x$endpoints[[2]], col="blue")

what: Alluvial diagram for multiple time series data — alluvial_ts
title: Alluvial diagram for multiple time series data — alluvial_ts
text: variant alluvial diagram suitable multiple (cross-sectional) time series. also works continuous variables equivalent time

title: Alluvial diagram for multiple time series data — alluvial_ts
code: alluvial_ts(   dat,   wave = NA,   ygap = 1,   col = NA,   alpha = NA,   plotdir = "up",   rankup = FALSE,   lab.cex = 1,   lab.col = "black",   xmargin = 0.1,   axis.col = "black",   title = NA,   title.cex = 1,   axis.cex = 1,   grid = FALSE,   grid.col = "grey80",   grid.lwd = 1,   leg.mode = TRUE,   leg.x = 0.1,   leg.y = 0.9,   leg.cex = 1,   leg.col = "black",   leg.lty = NA,   leg.lwd = NA,   leg.max = NA,   xlab = NA,   ylab = NA,   xlab.pos = 2,   ylab.pos = 1,   lwd = 1,   ... )

title: Alluvial diagram for multiple time series data — alluvial_ts
text: dat data.frame time-series (suitable equivalent continuously disaggregated data), 3 columns (order: category, time-variable, value) <= 1 row category-time combination wave numeric, curve wavyness defined terms x axis data range - .e. bezier point offset. Experiment get right ygap numeric, vertical distance polygons - multiple 10% mean data value col colour, value vector length matching number unique categories. Individual colours vector mapped categories alpha-numeric order alpha numeric, [0,1] polygon fill transparency plotdir character, string ('', '' 'centred') giving vertical alignment polygon stacks rankup logical, rank polygons time axes upward magnitude (largest smallest) lab.cex numeric, category label font size lab.col colour, category label xmargin numeric [0,1], proportional space category labels axis.col colour, axes title character, plot title title.cex numeric, plot title font size axis.cex numeric, font size x-axis break labels grid logical, plot vertical axes grid.col colour, grid axes grid.lwd numeric, line width grid axes leg.mode logical, draw y-axis scale legend inside largest data point (TRUE default) alternatively custom position/value (FALSE) leg.x, leg.y numeric [0,1], x/y positions legend leg.mode = FALSE leg.cex numeric, legend text size leg.col colour, legend lines text leg.lty numeric, code legend line type leg.lwd numeric, legend line width leg.max numeric, legend scale line width xlab, ylab character, x-axis / y-axis titles xlab.pos, ylab.pos numeric, perpendicular offset axis titles lwd numeric, value vector length matching number unique categories polygon stroke line width. Individual values vector mapped categories alpha-numeric order ... arguments pass polygon()

title: Alluvial diagram for multiple time series data — alluvial_ts
code: if( require(reshape2) ) { data(Refugees) reshape2::dcast(Refugees, country ~ year, value.var = 'refugees') d <- Refugees  set.seed(39) # for nice colours cols <- hsv(h = sample(1:10/10), s = sample(3:12)/15, v = sample(3:12)/15)  alluvial_ts(d) alluvial_ts(d, wave = .2, ygap = 5, lwd = 3) alluvial_ts(d, wave = .3, ygap = 5, col = cols) alluvial_ts(d, wave = .3, ygap = 5, col = cols, rankup = TRUE) alluvial_ts(d, wave = .3, ygap = 5, col = cols, plotdir = 'down') alluvial_ts(d, wave = .3, ygap = 5, col = cols, plotdir = 'centred', grid=TRUE,             grid.lwd = 5) alluvial_ts(d, wave =  0, ygap = 0, col = cols, alpha = .9, border = 'white',             grid = TRUE, grid.lwd = 5) alluvial_ts(d, wave = .3, ygap = 5, col = cols, xmargin = 0.4) alluvial_ts(d, wave = .3, ygap = 5, col = cols, xmargin = 0.3, lab.cex = .7) alluvial_ts(d, wave = .3, ygap = 5, col = cols, xmargin = 0.3, lab.cex=.7,             leg.cex=.7, leg.col = 'white') alluvial_ts(d, wave = .3, ygap = 5, col = cols, leg.mode = FALSE, leg.x = .1,             leg.y = .7, leg.max = 3e6) alluvial_ts(d, wave = .3, ygap = 5, col = cols, plotdir = 'centred', alpha=.9,             grid = TRUE, grid.lwd = 5, xmargin = 0.2, lab.cex = .7, xlab = '',             ylab = '', border = NA, axis.cex = .8, leg.cex = .7,             leg.col='white',           title = "UNHCR-recognised refugees\nTop 10 countries (2003-13)\n")  # non time-series example - Virginia deaths dataset d <- reshape2::melt(data.frame(age=row.names(VADeaths), VADeaths), id.vars='age')[,c(2,1,3)] names(d) = c('pop_group','age_group','deaths') alluvial_ts(d) } #> Loading required package: reshape2             #> [1] "Error: time variable must be numeric, factor, or ordered"
